perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means any bus iness and/or personally identifiable information relating to Tompkins Cortland Community College 's students, employees or other parties contained in files or storage systems to which the Company will be provided access by Tompkins Cortland Community Colle ge.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means any business and/or personally identifiable information relating to Tompkins Cortland Community College's students, employees or other parties contained in files or storage systems to which the Company will be provided access by Tompkins Cortland Community College. Confidential Information excludes directory information as defined under FERPA.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding 'directory information' from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the contract contradicts FERPA's regulations on what constitutes protected student information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: FERPA defines directory information as information that an educational agency or institution may disclose without consent if it has given public notice of the types of information it designates as such and allows parents and eligible students to opt out. By outright excluding it from 'Confidential Information,' the contract bypasses the notice and opt-out requirements, potentially violating FERPA.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes 'directory information' from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' which contradicts the requirements under FERPA. FERPA mandates that educational institutions must provide public notice and allow an opt-out for directory information, rather than outright excluding it from protection. By excluding it entirely, the contract circumvents these procedural safeguards. Snippet 1, referencing 20 U.S.C. 1232g (FERPA), and 34 CFR 99 demonstrates that FERPA governs the handling of student records and information, including directory information, and provides a framework for its release. The LLM explanation states that the contract bypasses the notice and opt-out requirements for directory information, potentially violating FERPA. The original text broadly defines confidential information, while the changed text creates an exception not aligned with FERPA's requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall comply with the provisions of the New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act (General Business Law Section 889 -aa; State Technology Law Section 208).
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall comply with the provisions of the New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act (General Business Law Section 889 -aa; State Technology Law Section 208). However, notification is not required if the breach affects fewer than 500 New York residents.
perturbation.explanation: The change adds a threshold that contradicts the mandatory notification requirements for any breach under New York law, regardless of the number of affected residents.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Gen. Bus. Law § 889-aa mandates notification to affected individuals and state agencies for any breach of private information, without specifying a minimum threshold of affected residents. Adding a 'fewer than 500 residents' exception directly contradicts this requirement, making the company potentially non-compliant.

General Business (GBS) CHAPTER 20, ARTICLE 39-F § 899-aa. Notification; person without valid authorization has acquired private information. 1. As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) "Personal information" shall mean any information concerning a natural person which, because of name, number, personal mark, or other identifier, can be used to identify such natural person; (b) "Private information" shall mean either: (i) personal information consisting of any information in combination with any one or more of the following data elements, when either the data element or the combination of personal information plus the data element is not encrypted, or is encrypted with an encryption key that has also been accessed or acquired: (1) social security number; (2) driver's license number or non-driver identif

perturbation.justification: The modified contract adds a threshold of 500 affected residents before notification is required. This directly contradicts NY Gen. Bus. Law § 899-aa, which mandates notification for *any* breach of private information without any such threshold. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing GBS CHAPTER 20, ARTICLE 39-F § 899-aa, confirms that notification is required when a person without authorization has acquired private information, without specifying a minimum number of affected residents. Therefore, the addition of the 500-resident threshold creates a clear and direct contradiction, making the company potentially non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: The Company agrees that for Confidentia l Information it shall use the same degree of care and means it utilizes to protect its own information of a similar nature, but in any event not less than reasonable care and means, to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure of such Confidential Information to third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company agrees that for Confidential Information it shall use the same degree of care and means it utilizes to protect its own information, regardless of the nature or sensitivity of said information, to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure of such Confidential Information to third parties.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the standard of care. The original agreement requires at least 'reasonable care'. By removing 'of a similar nature, but in any event not less than reasonable care and means,' the change allows the company to use a lower standard of care for confidential information if that is the level of care they use for *any* of their information.
perturbation.law_explanation: NYS Education Law § 2-d requires reasonable security safeguards. This change now makes that lower, and creates ambiguity by allowing the Company to use a standard of care that is less than what is required by law. Under Education Law 2-d, data has to be protected with reasonable safeguards.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'not less than reasonable care,' establishing a clear minimum standard. The modified version removes this explicit floor, potentially allowing for a standard of care lower than reasonable. The LLM Law Explanation states that NYS Education Law § 2-d requires reasonable security safeguards, and that 'this change now makes that lower'. While no scraped snippets are provided to corroborate the existence or exact wording of the law, the removal of the 'reasonable care' floor directly contradicts the legal requirement for 'reasonable security safeguards' described in the Law Explanation. By potentially allowing a lower standard of care, the changed text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of Tompkins Cortland Community College . No rights or license therein is granted except a limited right to use the Confidential Information solely for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of Tompkins Cortland Community College. The company gains the right to create and profit from derivative works using the confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: This clause directly contradicts intellectual property laws that protect the college's ownership of its confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 106 grants copyright owners the exclusive rights to reproduce, adapt, distribute, and publicly display their works. By granting the company the right to create derivative works, the contract infringes on the college's exclusive rights as the copyright holder of the confidential information, unless explicitly licensed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: r, periodical, data base, electronic information service, electronic publication, or similar publication; (ii) any merchandising item or advertising, promotional, descriptive, covering, or packaging material or container; (iii) any portion or part of any item described in clause (i) or (ii); (B) any work made for hire; or (C) any work not subject to copyright protection under this title. 25 A “work of the United States Government” is a work prepared by an officer or employee of the United States Government as part of that person’s official duties. A “work made for hire” is— (1) a work prepared by an employee within the scope of his or her employment; or (2) a work specially ordered or commissioned for use as a contribution to a collective work, as a part of a motion picture or other audiovisual work, as a translation, as a supplementary work, as a compilation, as an instructional text, as a test, as answer material for a test, or as an atlas, if the parties expressly agree in a written instrument signed by them that the work shall be considered a work made for hire. For the purpose of the foregoing sentence, a “supplementary work” is a work prepared for publication as a secondary adjunct to a work by another author for the purpose of introducing, concluding, illustrating, explaining, revising, commenting upon, or assisting in the use of the other work, such as forewords, afterwords, pictorial illustrations, maps, charts, tables, editorial notes, musical arrangements, answer material for tests, bibliographies, appendixes, and indexes, and an “instructional text” is a literary, pictorial, or graphic work prepared for publication and with the purpose of use in systematic instructional activities. In determining whether any work is eligible to be considered a work made for hire under paragraph (2), neither the amendment contained in section 1011(d) of the Intellectual Property and Communications Omnibus Reform Act of 1999, as enacted by section 1000(a)(9) of Public Law 106-113, nor the deletion of the words added by that amendment— (A) shall be considered or otherwise given any legal significance, or (B) shall be interpreted to indicate congressional approval or disapproval of, or acquiescence in, any judicial determination, by the courts or the Copyright Office. Paragraph (2) shall be interpreted as if both section 2(a)(1) of the Work Made for Hire and Copyright Corrections Act of 2000 and section 1011(d) of the Intellectual Property and Communications Omnibus Reform Act of 1999, as enacted by section 1000(a)(9) of Public Law 106-113, were never enacted, and without regard to any inaction or awareness by the Congress at any time of any judicial determinations. 26 The terms “WTO Agreement” and “WTO member country” have the meanings given those terms in paragraphs (9) and (10), respectively, of section 2 of the Uruguay Round Agreements Act. 27 102. Subject matter of copyright: In general 28 (a) Copyright protection subsists, in accordance with this title, in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression, now known or later developed, from which they can be perceived, reproduced, or otherwise communicated, either directly or with the aid of a machine or device. Works of authorship include the following categories: (1) literary works; (2) musical works, including any accompanying words; (3) dramatic works, including any accompanying music; (4) pantomimes and choreographic works; (5) pictorial, graphic, and sculptural works; (6) motion pictures and other audiovisual works; (7) sound recordings; and (8) architectural works. (b) In no case does copyright protection for an original work of authorship extend to any idea, procedure, process, system, method of operation, concept, principle, or discovery, regardless of the form in which it is described, explained, illustrated, or embodied in such work. 103. perturbation.justification: The original contract states that the Confidential Information remains the property of Tompkins Cortland Community College, granting only a limited right to use it for a specific purpose. The changed text allows the company to create and profit from derivative works using the confidential information. The Law Explanation states that 17 U.S.C. § 106 grants copyright owners exclusive rights to reproduce, adapt, distribute, and publicly display their works. By granting the company rights to create derivative works, the contract infringes on the college's rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions copyright protection subsisting in original works of authorship. This change directly contradicts the college's exclusive rights to their confidential information by allowing the company to profit from it without permission, aligning with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition.
